doc_title: Natural hazard mitigation plan Jefferson County
text:
The Federal Emergency Management Agency defines
hazard mitigation as an action intended to reduce
repetitive losses from future natural disasters.
Counties, cities and towns are required to prepare a
hazard mitigation plan in order to qualify to receive
Hazard Mitigation Grant Program project grants in
the event of a disaster. These requirements are
found in Section 322 of the Disaster Mitigation Act of
2000 and section 409 of the Robert T. Stafford
Disaster Relief and Emergency Assistance Act (Public
Law 93-288). An excerpt of this Act follows:
Hazard Mitigation
Sec. 404. (a) In General. The President may
contribute up to 75 percent of the cost of hazard
mitigation measures which the President has
determined are cost-effective and which substantially
reduce the risk of future damage, hardship, loss, or
suffering in any area affected by a major disaster.
Such measures shall be identified following the
evaluation of natural hazards under section 409 and
shall be subject to approval by the President.
The plan was developed in an open public
involvement process that included opportunities for
the public to comment on the plan, for other
agencies and interests to be involved in the planning
and incorporation of existing plans, studies, reports
and technical information. The plan is to be updated
annually and following a presidential disaster
declaration.
The plan itself has these chapters:
1. Documentation of the planning process.
2. A risk assessment that proVides the factual
basis for activities proposed in the strategy
to reduce losses from identified hazards.
3. A mitigation strategy that provides the
blueprint for reducing the potential losses
identified in the risk assessment.
4. A plan maintenance process.
5. Documentation that the plan has been
formally adopted by the governing bodies of
the jurisdictions.

doc_title: Natural hazard mitigation plan Jefferson County
text:
Jefferson County Hazard Mitigation
Working Group
The Jefferson County Hazard Mitigation Working
Group was formed to provide gUidance during the
preparation of this Plan. This Group was comprised
of various interest groups from local city government,
county commissioners, rural fire departments, and
local businesses. They identified natural hazards that
might affect the county by killing or injuring persons
or by destroying property and by disrupting activities.
The Group formulated a set of hazard mitigation
goals, which are listed in priority order.
Hazard Mitigation Goals
1. Protection from loss of life and personal
injury.
2. Protection of critical facilities and
infrastructure.
3. Protection of personal property and
reduction of economic injury due to hazards.
4. Minimize the costs of disaster response.
For each hazard, this question was asked: Are there
practical ways that the hazard be altered, avoided,
averted, adapted to so as to mitigate its harmful
effects? Practicality was measured through
consideration of the cost of the action in conjunction
with the vulnerability, potential losses and frequency
of occurrence of the hazard.
The Group also identified and suggested funding
sources for hazard mitigation measure in each
vulnerable area based on input from government
agencies, consultants and citizens.
Public Involvement
An effort was made to solicit public input during the
planning process and the public meetings were held
during the formation of the Plan; one at the
beginning, one after a first draft was produced, and
one after a final draft was produced. Feedback
received from the public proved valuable in the
development of the Plan. Several comments were
received that led to the re-thinking of some proposed
priority mitigation actions.

doc_title: Natural hazard mitigation plan Jefferson County
text:
2.1 - Types of Natural Hazards
The 13 natural hazards affecting the county have
been identified as being from three categories.
1. Weather Hazards
o Flooding
o General Rain Floods
o Flash Floods
o Thunderstorms
o Tornados
o Severe Thunderstorm Winds
o Hail
o Lightning
o Ice/Snow Storms
o Extreme Heat
o Drought
2. Geologic Hazards
o Earthquake
o Expansive Soils
3. Technological Hazards
o Wildfires
o Dam Failure
This natural hazard identification section is to give
the reader some idea of how vulnerable the residents
of Jefferson County are to 13 natural hazard events.
The format is:
o Identify the topic natural hazard.
o List some facts pertinent to the nature of the
hazard.
o Other observations that enhance understanding
of the hazard.
o List in an appendix some descriptions of hazards
events that are representative of what has or
could happen in the county.

WEATHER HAZARD-FLOODING
Flooding is Oklahoma's most frequent and most
costly natural hazard. Thirteen flood events were
recorded in Jefferson County since 1992. Even
though Jefferson County is about 400 miles from the
Gulf Coast, tropical storms can still create general
rain flooding by causing heavy rain to fall for many
hours. During the spring and fall flash floods can
result from thunderstorms.
general rain flood - Accumulation of water
within a water body and the overflow of excess
water onto adjacent floodplain lands, characterized
by a slow steady rise in stream stage and a peak
flood of long duration.
floodplain - Land adjoining the channel of a river,
stream or lake that is susceptible to flooding.
Flood Facts
o Flood emergencies were 71% (34) of the federal
disaster declarations in Oklahoma between June
1995 and December 31, 2002.
o Few lives are lost to general rain floods, but
much property is damaged.
o Standard homeowners and commercial property
policies do not cover flood losses. These
properties can be insured for flood losses only
through the National Flood Insurance Program
offered by the federal government.
o The National Flood Insurance Program is not
available to residents of rural Jefferson County at
this time.
flash flood - Flood characterized by a qUick water rise
level of short duration.
Flash Flood Facts
o Flash floods are the second greatest killer among
natural hazards in Oklahoma.
o All of the 11 flood events recorded in Jefferson
County since 1993 are characterized as flash
floods.

doc_title: Natural hazard mitigation plan Jefferson County
text:
thunderstorm - A transient storm of lightning and
thunder, usually with rain and gusty winds.
Thunderstorm Facts
o The National Weather Service classifies a
thunderstorm as severe if:
o Wind speeds reach 58 mph or greater
o A tornado is produced
o Surface hail of 0.75" or greater is
dropped
o Severe thunderstorms occur predominately
between March 15 and June 15 each year.
o Thunderstorms are equally likely in all locations.
o Thunderstorms are deadly and damaging
because they produce
o Tornados
o Thunderstorm Winds
o Hail
o Lightning
o An average thunderstorm is 15 miles in diameter
and lasts about 30 minutes.
Tornados
tornado - A localized, violently destructive windstorm
characterized by a funnel-shaped cloud that extends
to the ground and marks the path of the greatest
intensity.
Tornado Facts
o Tornados are the number one thunderstorm
killer in Oklahoma.
o Tornado damage is described according to the Fujita Tornado Scale.
| Category | Wind Speed | Damage Description |
|---|---|---|
| F0 | 40–72 mph | Light damage |
| F1 | 73–112 mph | Moderate damage |
| F2 | 113–157 mph | Considerable damage – houses destroyed |
| F3 | 158–206 mph | Severe damage |
| F4 | 207–260 mph | Devastating damage |
| F5 | 261–318 mph | Incredible damage – houses swept clear |
o The stronger the tornado, the more minutes a warning system has time to work.  
Example: The Bridgecreek/Moore multi-vortex tornado began north of Stillwater at 4 PM with F0 damage; then struck Moore at 6:12 PM with F5 damage.
o Timely warning of an approaching tornado can reduce casualties and injuries.
Storm shelters and safe rooms are effective in preventing loss of life and injuries.
o National Climatic Data Center statistics recorded 359 significant tornado days in Oklahoma since 1950. The strongest storm on each of those days was:
- F2 – 259 days (69%)
- F3 – 82 days (21%)
- F4 – 43 days (9%)
- F5 – 5 days (1%)
o National Climatic Data Center storm event statistics record 5 significant tornado days in Jefferson County since 1950. On those 5 days, 6 tornados killed one person and caused injury to 17 others and did $4.6 million of property damage.
o The map shows the National Severe Storms Laboratory estimates of the number of days a significant tornado will touch down within 25 miles of any location in 100 years.
o The map value for Jefferson County is about 35, or 0.168 significant tornados per square mile (35 / 25² × 3.1416, the area of a circle with a 25 mile radius).

doc_title: Natural hazard mitigation plan Jefferson County
section: Why Were So Many More People killed In the Olden Days than Now?
text:
The short answer is improved building methods, rescue efforts, medical attention and tornado warnings. At the turn of the 20th century there was no such thing as poured concrete. It was not possible to bolt down houses. They just sat on stone foundations, and flew away with people in them. Modern building codes that require better construction, but mobile homes still offer relatively little protection. There were also no "trauma" units, no rescue squads, and no antibiotics. People who were mangled and crushed developed infections, gangrene, and tetanus. Sometimes they lingered for weeks before death. Nowadays, if a tornado victim survives the first day or so, it is very likely that they will recover fully. How homes are heated is also a factor. If a house collapsed in the olden days, the overturned wood cook stove might catch fire and burn the occupants of the home to death before rescue workers even knew they were trapped in the house. If the home was in a city, the adjoining homes often caught fire. Prior to the 1920's, there was no such thing as commercial radio, and much less awareness of what was going on over the horizon. Tornado watches are now issued so far in advance of the actual onset of bad weather that spotters can be mobilized, and television weathermen geared up for a live broadcast of a tornado in progress. Television weathermen break into scheduled programming to point out the exact position of a storm whether you want to know it or not! Even at night in the middle of a raging storm, it is still possible to keep totally aware of whether tornadoes are any threat at all.
In order to provide an understanding of the extent of potential damage by a tornado, the average damage path of five tornados that caused F2 damage during the May 3, 1999 outbreak in Oklahoma was mapped. A diagram of the damage footprint is shown below. The damage footprint is the outer limit of F1 damage. The F1 damage area is 6 miles long and has a maximum width of 1500 feet. Single family structures and apartment units are expected to incur a 20% loss inside the F1 level contour.
Commercial structures are expected, on average, to be more durable than single family structures, while mobile homes will be less durable. Inside the F1 damage area is smaller area were F2 damage occurs. This F2 damage area is 2 miles long and has a maximum width of 750 feet, an area of about 160 acres or 0.25 square miles. Applying the NSSL map value of 0.0168 significant tornados days per square mile per 100 years to the 874 square mile area of Jefferson County provides an estimate of 16 significant tornados days per 100 years. Combining the F2 damage area with the estimated number of tornados by multiplication provides an estimate of the probability of F2 or greater tornado damage within the next 100 years at any location. That probability is 0.45%.
section_path: Why Were So Many More People killed In the Olden Days than Now?

doc_title: Natural hazard mitigation plan Jefferson County
section: High Thunderstorm Wind AND Lighting AND Hail
text:
High Thunderstorm Wind
wind - Air in motion, as along the earth's surface.
Wind Facts
o National Climatic Data Center storm event
statistics record 60 high thunderstorm wind
events in Jefferson County during the 20-year
period ending June 30, 2003. Three persons
were injured in those events. Reported
damage was $6.2 million.
o The same mitigation action as for tornadoes can
be employed for thunderstorm as well.
o Thunderstorms with damaging winds are usually
part of a line of thunderstorms that can be seen
on weather radar.
o Buildings and houses constructed in accordance
with modern bUilding codes should withstand
most straight-line winds from thunderstorms.
Lightning
lightning - A luminous, electrical discharge in the
atmosphere caused by the electric-charge separation
of precipitation particles within a cumulonimbus, or
thunderstorm, cloud.
Lightning Facts
o Lightning killed 88 people and injured 243 others
in Oklahoma during the period 1959-1994.
o Secondary effects from lightning include fires,
power disruption, and damage to objects struck
by the flash.
o While the onset of a particular lightning strike is
instantaneous, a thunderstorm cloud is always
present.
o Usually a storm with cloud to ground lightning
can be seen approaching.
o Lightning heats the surrounding air to greater
than 50,000 degrees Fahrenheit. The qUick
heating and cooling of the air causes a shock
wave which produces thunder.
Hail
hail - Showery precipitation in the form of irregular
pellets or balls of ice, falling from a thunderstorm.
Hail Facts
o National Climatic Data Center statistics record
1.5 inch or larger hail events in the Jefferson
County on 29 days during the 20 year period
ending June 30, 2003.
o Hail forms when water droplets freeze and thaw
as they are carried up and down in updrafts and
down- drafts in thunderstorms.
o Hailstorms seldom injure people.
o Roofs, vehicles and crops are what is commonly
damaged by hail.
o Hail is responsible for nearly $1 billion in damage
to crops and property each year in the U.S.
o Most hail is less than 1.5 inches in diameter.
section_path: High Thunderstorm Wind AND Lighting AND Hail

doc_title: Natural hazard mitigation plan Jefferson County
section: WEATHER HAZARD-WINTER STORM AND EXTREME HEAT
text:
WEATHER HAZARD-WINTER STORM
blizzard - A heavy prolonged snowstorm, covering a
wide area.
Cold Spots
o Winter storms are equally likely in all locations.
o Almost every winter storm that visits the state
leaves a bit of ice/snow. The ordinary rain-sleetsnow event causes transportation problems, but
doesn't do direct damage.
o Ice/snow storms caused by freezing rain are the
greatest winter weather hazard.
o Electric power is disrupted in cities for
days; in rural area for weeks.
o Vital services such as water and sewer
systems must use generators t continue
to function.
o Downed trees, power lines and poles
close roadways.
o The cost or clearing debris is huge.
o The typical major ice/snow storm event footprint
is 30 miles on either side of a 150 long line
running from southwest to northeast covering
9000 square miles.
Highway Icing
Every year (almost) the highways are covered with
sleet, snow or ice during a winter storm. This is the
primary contributing cause to a lot of traffic accidents
with injuries and fatalities. It is a particular
aggravation to school superintendents as they must
decide on school closings for weather, usually while
the road conditions at the time the school busses
would be making their routes is uncertain. It is also a
big a problem to all route drivers and to commuters.

WEATHER HAZARD-EXTREME HEAT
Hot Spots
o Extreme heat is a summer phenomena.
o Extreme temperatures are slightly higher in the
southwest part of the county.
o Heat stroke, heat exhaustion and heat cramps
are the major human risks from exposure to
extreme heat.
o Extreme heat is life threatening to elderly and
frail persons who live alone.
o Extreme heat diminishes water supplies and
increases fire danger.
o Peak daily electricity use spikes when people run
air conditioners and fans during a heat wave.
o Roads, bridges and railroad tracks may suffer
damage from extreme heat conditions.
o The highest recorded temperature in Oklahoma
was 120 F at Tipton.
section_path: WEATHER HAZARD-WINTER STORM AND EXTREME HEAT

doc_title: Natural hazard mitigation plan Jefferson County
text:
Drought – An extended period of dry weather, especially one injurious to crops.

Drought Facts

- Droughts are usually associated with long periods of warm, dry summer days, but droughts can occur and cause harm during all seasons.
- Drought is equally likely in all locations.
- When drought begins, the agricultural sector is the first to be affected.
- Those who rely on lakes and groundwater are the last to be affected by drought.
- The most dramatic drought periods occurred in the 1930s and 1950s when Oklahoma was affected by drought for years at a time.

The Legacy of the 1930s Drought

The magnitude of the droughts of the 1930s, combined with the Great Depression, led to unprecedented government relief efforts. Congressional actions in 1934 alone accounted for relief expenditures of $525 million; the total cost (social, economic, and environmental) would be impossible to determine.

This era ushered in some of the first long-term programs to reduce future vulnerability to drought. It was in these years that the Soil Conservation Service—now the Natural Resources Conservation Service—began to stress soil conservation measures.

The first soil conservation districts came into being, and demonstration projects were carried out to show the benefits of practices such as terracing and contouring.

The measures continued in the years following the drought: conservation practices and irrigation increased, crop diversity increased, federal crop insurance was established, and the regional economy was diversified. Other measures taken after the 1930s drought also reduced rural and urban vulnerability to drought, including new or enlarged reservoirs, improved domestic water systems, changes in farm policies, new insurance and aid programs, and removal of some of the most sensitive agricultural lands from production.

Types of Drought

| Type | Description |
|---|---|
| Agricultural | Based on soil moisture deficiencies relative to water demands of plant life. |
| Hydrologic | Based on precipitation shortfalls on streamflows and reservoir, lake, and groundwater levels. |
| Meteorologic | Based on degree of dryness; actual precipitation is less than expected normal amount. |
| Socioeconomic | Occurs when the demand for water exceeds supply due to a weather-related supply shortfall. |

doc_title: Natural hazard mitigation plan Jefferson County
text:
Geological Hazard – Earthquake

Earthquake – A vibration or movement of a part of the earth’s surface due to the faulting of rocks, volcanic forces, etc.

Earthquake Facts

- Magnitude and intensity are used to describe earthquakes. Each earthquake has one magnitude. Intensity differs throughout the area.
- Events are considered notable if the magnitude was greater than M5.
- Deaths usually occur due to structural damages.
- About 50 small earthquakes are located in the state each year, with only one to three of these being felt.
- Oklahoma Geological Survey's Oklahoma Seismograph Network located nine earthquakes in Jefferson County from 1977 through 2002.
- Atmospheric conditions have caused a “time on target” simultaneous impacts at Fort Sill to be heard as far away as Oklahoma City. Except for a tiny signal at the seismograph in Meers, there were no detectable seismic vibrations.

A report from near the epicenter of a magnitude 3.8 earthquake that occurred February 8, 2002. Location is 5 miles west and 1 mile north of Elgin.

“I was sitting at my desk in our home office doing paper work and listening to Ft. Sill practice bombing. Then I heard a bombing that lasted four times longer and louder when the shaking started and it seemed that it would never stop. At first I thought Ft. Sill had put too much dynamite into the last bomb but it just kept getting louder and the shaking started. Our house shakes when Ft. Sill is practicing but you get used to it. This you could definitely tell was not the base. My whole desk was shaking as well as myself in the chair. The entire house shook for at least a minute startling my daughter. Nothing fell off the walls but the chandelier was swaying in the living room where my daughter was and some of the pictures on the wall were crooked. I did not notice anything before this quake but I told my daughter that this one felt a lot stronger than the one I felt in 1998 at this same location. The one in 1998 was like a rubber band one or two shakes back and forth but it was just as loud.”

Magnitude 2.0+ Earthquake events in Jefferson County since 1977

| Year | Month | Day | Magnitude | Latitude | Longitude |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1980 | AUG | 05 | 2.2 | 34.0960 | -97.5880 |
| 1985 | NOV | 07 | 2.0 | 34.1810 | -97.7900 |
| 1995 | DEC | 14 | 2.3 | 34.0880 | -97.9830 |
| 2002 | MAR | 12 | 2.2 | 34.2701 | -97.5299 |
| 2002 | APR | 19 | 2.0 | 34.0486 | -97.7632 |
| 2002 | MAY | 31 | 3.0 | 34.9997 | -97.6228 |
| 2002 | JUL | 09 | 2.5 | 34.0035 | -97.5631 |

doc_title: Natural hazard mitigation plan Jefferson County
text:
GEOLOGICAL HAZARD -EXPANSIVE SOILS
expansive soils - Soils that increase in volume
as they get wet and shrink as they dry out.
EXPANSIVESOIL FACTS
o Soils that expand have a high proportion of
water-adsorbing clay particles. Expansive soils
present no problem in their natural state.
o Expansive soils are one of the nation's most
prevalent causes of damage to bUildings and
construction.
o Most damage occurs to roads and structures
built on expansive soils.
o Water and sewer lines, gas pipelines, driveways and sidewalks are also damaged.
o Methods to mitigate expansive soil damage are
well developed, although more costly initially.
TECHNOLOGICAL HAZARD-WILDFIRE
wildfire - An open fire which spreads
unconstrained through the countryside.If no t
controlled, wildfire will threaten property and
lives.
Fire Facts
o There are three categories of wildfires:
Wildland fire - fuel is mainly natural
vegetation.
Interface fire - urban/wildland fires, both
vegetation and manmade fuel.
Prescribed fire-fire purposefully set and
allowed to burn.
o Over 90% of the fires to which rural and small
town fire departments respond to are wildfires.
o There are 1 rural fire department and 6 small
town fire departments in Jefferson County.
o The worst outbreak of wildfires in recent
Oklahoma history occurred in 1996.
o Fuel, weather and topography are the three
main factors that affect wildfires.
o The particularly dangerous times of year for
wildfire are late winter and late summer.

doc_title: Natural hazard mitigation plan Jefferson County
text:
dam - A manmade barrier, together with appurtenant
structures, constructed above the natural surface of
the ground for the purpose of impounding water.
Dam Safety
o There has never been a dam failure in
Oklahoma.
o Causes of dam failures:
• Excessive runoff after unusually heavy
rainfall.
Inadequate spillway capacity, which can
cause over topping
Old age and inadequate maintenance.
• Earthquakes.
o The Oklahoma Water Resources Board
coordinates a state dam safety program.
o 21 dams exist in Jefferson County, of which one
is classed as significant hazard structure and one
is a high-hazard dam.
OWRB Dam Safety Program
The Oklahoma Water Resources Board coordinates a
state dam safety program that ensures the safety of
more than 4,500 dams in the state, with a priority
placed on the protection of downstream life and
property. The program requires inspections every five
and three years for low and significant hazard
structures, respectively. It requires annual inspection
of the state's 165 high-hazard dams, so designated
due to the presence of occupied dwellings
immediately downstream. Because many of these
dams are very old structures and, as a result, require
periodic repair, the Water Board requires submittal
and subsequent approval of plans and specifications
prior to dam modifications. Staff also coordinates
periodic training sessions and workshops on dam
safety issues and regulations for dam owners and
engineers.

|---|---|---|---|---|

| Hazard Type | General Rain Flooding | Flash Floods | Winter Storms | Extreme Heat |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Specific Hazard | General Rain Flooding | Flash Floods | Ice/snow accumulation | Heat wave |
| How bad can it get? | 500 year flood | Water covering roads and flooding buildings | Ice/snow accumulation of 2 in. | 30 consecutive days of 100+ degree high temperatures |
| Expected Event | 100 year flood | Water covering roads and flooding buildings | Ice/snow accumulation of 0.5 in. | 10 consecutive days of 100+ degree high temperatures |
| Where can it occur? | Along rivers | Along any stream or drainage channel | Anywhere in the county | Anywhere in the county |
| How often is it likely to occur? | Once in 100 years | Once each year | Once in 2 years | Once in 5 years |
| What is the speed of onset? | Hours and days | Minutes and hours | Hours and days | Days |
| Harm to Persons | People evacuated | Drowning of motorist | Death or injury in highway accident | Frail persons who live alone die |
| Harm to Structures | Extensive property damage | Damage to flooded structures | Electric lines down | None |
| Other effects | Emergency response units unable to reach emergency scene | Emergency response units unable to reach emergency scene | Power outages for days | — |
| Hazard Type | Drought | Earthquake | Expansive Soils | Dam Failure | Wildfire |

doc_title: Natural hazard mitigation plan Jefferson County
text:
This section describes vulnerability in terms of the types and numbers of existing and future buildings, infrastructure, and critical facilities located in hazard areas and an estimate of the potential dollar losses to vulnerable structures identified.

Jefferson County Critical Facilities
| Structure | Number | Estimated Value |
|---|---|---|
| Courthouse | 1 | $20 million |
| County barns | 3 | $600,000 |
| County Hospital | 1 | $25 million |
| Roads and bridges | — | Not estimated |
The estimated value of housing was prepared from census data. Owner-occupied units were assigned the median value of owner occupied housing in the place or census tract. Renter occupied units were assigned 100 times the median value of gross rent. Vacant housing units were assumed to be either owner or rented in the same proportion as owner or rented housing units. The resulting estimated housing value is $27,419.

doc_title: Natural hazard mitigation plan Jefferson County
text:
Hydrologic Hazard – Flooding

The hazard area considered to be vulnerable due to flooding is that inside the boundary of a 100-year frequency flood. Roads subject to overflow during flash floods are a hazard to travelers.

Flood Insurance Policy Statistics as of 12/31/2002

| Community Name | Policies In Force | Insurance In Force |
|---|---|---|
| Waurika | 16 | $1,127,100 |

To identify all of the buildings inside the boundary of a 100-year frequency flood, the boundary as shown on the FEMA flood maps was digitized into an ArcView map layer. That map layer was superimposed over newest available aerial photographs of Jefferson County. The resulting map was visually searched for buildings. This search found one home and no other buildings in rural Jefferson County inside the 100-year flood boundary. The county courthouse in Waurika is inside the 100-year flood boundary.

None of Jefferson County's critical facilities are within the 100-year flood boundary. However, the county road system is subject to overflow in several places during flash floods. The total number of places that county roads are subject to damage during heavy rains is so large as to be practically uncountable.

Vulnerability to Flooding

| Structure | Number | Estimated Value | Potential Loss |
|---|---|---|---|
| Housing units | 2 | $54,838 | $27,419 |
| Other buildings | 0 | $0 | $0 |
| Critical facilities | 1 | $20 million | $1 million |
| Infrastructure | 0 | Not estimated | Not estimated |

The map shows the flood hazard areas within Jefferson County.

doc_title: Natural hazard mitigation plan Jefferson County
section: Weather Hazard – Thunderstorm AND High Winds
text:
Weather Hazard – Thunderstorm

Tornados

The entire county is equally vulnerable to tornado damage.

Vulnerability to F2 or greater Tornados

| Structure | Number | Estimated Value | Damage Rate | Potential Damage | Expected Loss (0.42%) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Housing units | 952 | $26.1 million | 80% | $20.9 million | $88,000 |
| Other buildings | Not estimated | — | — | — | — |
| Critical facilities | 5 | $45.6 million | 20% | $9.1 million | $27,000 |
| Infrastructure | Not estimated | — | — | — | — |

Damage path of one F2 tornado. Approximately 16 are expected in 100 years within the county.

High Winds

The entire county is equally vulnerable to wind damage during severe thunderstorms. Severe thunderstorms are so common that the probability of damaging winds (80 mph or greater) within the next 100 years approaches 100% at any location. Single family structures and apartment units built to current building code standards can withstand 80 mph wind with minor damage. An estimated 50% of existing housing units are built to those standards.

Vulnerability to Thunderstorm High Winds

| Structure | Number | Estimated Value | Damage Rate | Potential Damage | Expected Loss (50%) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Housing units | 952 | $45.6 million | 10% | $4.6 million | $2.3 million |
| Other buildings | Not estimated | — | — | — | — |
| Critical facilities | 4 | $45.6 million | 0% | $0 | $0 |
| Infrastructure | Not estimated | — | — | — | — |
section_path: Weather Hazard – Thunderstorm AND High Winds

doc_title: Natural hazard mitigation plan Jefferson County
section: Weather Hazard – Thunderstorm: Hail and Lightning
text:
Hail

The entire county is equally vulnerable to hail damage during severe thunderstorms. Severe thunderstorms are so common that the probability of damaging hail (1.5 in. or greater) within the next 100 years approaches 100% at any location. Only the very strongest of buildings can withstand 1.5 in. or larger hail without damage to the roof. No estimate of potential hail damage is made.

Lightning

The entire county is equally vulnerable to lightning damage during severe thunderstorms. Lightning is a deadly hazard to persons, animals and anything electric, but the damage footprint for any strike is small. No estimate of potential lightning damage is made.
section_path: Weather Hazard – Thunderstorm: Hail and Lightning

doc_title: Natural hazard mitigation plan Jefferson County
section: Geological Hazard – Earthquake AND Winter Storm
text:
Geological Hazard – Earthquake

Jefferson County has no history of earthquake damage. Earthquakes of the magnitude (M5) and intensity expected will not cause significant damage to buildings, infrastructure and critical facilities.

The map shows recorded earthquake epicenters in the region.

The entire county is equally vulnerable to winter storms. Winter storms down electric power lines, which can disrupt services and emergency communications at the county courthouse and county barns. Winter storms cover roads with ice, which makes travel dangerous. Buildings are seldom damaged by snow and ice.

No estimate of potential winter storm damage is made.
section_path: Geological Hazard – Earthquake AND Winter Storm

doc_title: Natural hazard mitigation plan Jefferson County
section: Technological Hazard – Wildfire AND Wildfire AND Dam Failure AND Expansive Soils
text:
Technological Hazard – Wildfire

Almost 30% of Jefferson County soils will support plants that create high fuel load for wildfire. No estimate of potential wildfire damage is made.

The map shows areas of high wildfire fuel soils within Jefferson County.

Technological Hazard – Dam Failure

Two dams in Jefferson County are classed as significant or high hazard structures. Failure of the Waurika Lake dam would cause flooding in Waurika and at the County Courthouse. The same is true, with much smaller effect, of the Cow Creek Site 1A dam.

The greater impact of the failure of the Waurika Lake dam would be the loss of the public water supply storage space. The terms “high hazard” and “significant hazard” refer to the damage that would result from a dam break, not the integrity of the dam itself.

The map identifies the locations of significant and high hazard dams including Waurika Lake and Cow Creek Site 1A.

Geological Hazard – Expansive Soils

Some soils in Jefferson County are expansive. Structures built on these soils are vulnerable to damage due to expansion and contraction of the soil. This type of damage occurs gradually instead of in a single event. No estimate is made of potential dollar losses to existing buildings.

The map shows the distribution of expansive soils within Jefferson County.
section_path: Technological Hazard – Wildfire AND Wildfire AND Dam Failure AND Expansive Soils

doc_title: Natural hazard mitigation plan Jefferson County
section: Weather Hazards – Extreme Heat and Drought AND 2.2(iii) Land Uses and Development Trends
text:
Weather Hazard – Extreme Heat

Extreme heat will not cause significant damage to buildings and critical facilities. It does occasionally damage roads.

Weather Hazard – Drought

Drought will not cause significant damage to buildings, infrastructure and critical facilities.

2.2(iii) Land Uses and Development Trends
Current Land Use

Jefferson County has a total land area of approximately 559,360 acres, or 874 square miles. The entire county would be classed as rural. Transportation, urban and miscellaneous lands total 18,560 acres leaving 367,300 acres as agricultural land. Of this amount about 83 acres is in permanent grasses and 173,500 acres in cropland.

Development Trends

The small cities and towns have been growing slowly, increasing land area while development density is decreasing as fewer people live in each house and new construction happens at the edge of the locality. In the balance of the county the trend is toward larger agricultural operations and fewer houses. The availability of domestic water from rural water districts has made rural homes practical in areas of Jefferson County where ground water is scarce or not drinkable.
section_path: Weather Hazards – Extreme Heat and Drought AND 2.2(iii) Land Uses and Development Trends

doc_title: Natural hazard mitigation plan Jefferson County
section: 3 – Hazard Mitigation Strategy AND 3.1 – Mitigation Goals
text:
3 – Hazard Mitigation Strategy

This chapter provides the blueprint for reducing the geographic areas where potential losses identified in the risk assessment can occur. The strategy is based on existing authorities, policies, programs and capabilities that can expand and improve these existing tools.

Implementation of hazard mitigation strategies in Jefferson County are shared between the county government and the municipalities. The municipalities have primary responsibility for hazard mitigation inside their geographic areas. The county government as a subdivision of the state government has responsibility for the broad emergency management functions throughout the entire county, especially for actions that are not site specific, such as dissemination of storm warnings, and for tasks that overwhelm the capacity of localities, such as rescue coordination and removal of storm debris.

3.1 – Mitigation Goals

1. Protection from loss of life and personal injury.
2. Protection of critical facilities and infrastructure.
3. Protection of personal property and reduction of economic injury due to hazards.
4. Minimize the costs of disaster response.
section_path: 3 – Hazard Mitigation Strategy AND 3.1 – Mitigation Goals

doc_title: Natural hazard mitigation plan Jefferson County
text:
3.2 – Mitigation Approaches

For each hazard the question was asked:
Are there practical ways to:
- alter (act on) the hazard,
- avert (redirect) the hazard,
- adapt to (interact with) the hazard, and/or
- avoid the hazard?

Alter the Hazard – The hazard can be acted upon (altered) in such a manner as to reduce or even eliminate the frequency and intensity of the occurrence. Example: reducing fuel for ignition sources in wildland/urban interface areas.

Avert the Hazard – This does not eliminate the hazard; it redirects the impacts of the hazard away from vulnerable areas. There are both structural and nonstructural methods. Structural methods may include building a levee to redirect water flow. A nonstructural example would be vegetation management to redirect runoff.

Adapt to the Hazard – This involves reducing vulnerability to a hazard. Examples include retrofitting structures to be more resistant to damage from hail, high winds, fires, or building safe rooms for protection from tornados.

Avoid the Hazard – Remove or do not place people and structures in risk areas. An example is to create open spaces and restrict building in areas most prone to flooding.

Practicality was measured through consideration of the cost of the action in conjunction with the vulnerability, potential losses and frequency of occurrence of the hazard.

Flooding

Goal 1. Protection from loss of life and personal injury
- Warning the public of an impending flood or water over roadways
- Replace low water crossings with bridges
- Raise roads subject to flooding

Goal 2. Protection of critical facilities and infrastructure
- Flood proof critical facilities and facilities in flood hazard areas

Goal 3. Protection of personal property and reduction of economic injury due to hazard
- Floodplain management to advise builders of flood hazards and restrict or require elevation of new structures in flood zones
- Waterway alteration
- Storm sewers to carry runoff away
- Acquire and demolish or relocate existing flood prone structures
- Land acquisition
- Wetland Creation/Mitigation/Restoration

doc_title: Natural hazard mitigation plan Jefferson County
section: Tornados and High Thunderstorm Winds AND Lightning
text:
Tornados and High Thunderstorm Winds

Goal 1. Protection from loss of life and personal injury
- Warning the public of possible tornado or severe thunderstorm winds
- Provide public shelter storm shelters in places such as schools, nursing homes, employment places and mobile home parks
- Public education on the most effective shelters
- Encourage storm shelters in homes
Goal 2. Protection of critical facilities & infrastructure
- Retrofit or protect critical facilities preventing severe weather damage

Goal 3. Protection of personal property and reduction of economic injury due to hazards
- Construction standards
- Reconstruction standards

Lightning

Goal 1. Protection from loss of life and personal injury
- Stopping outside events when cloud to ground lightning is anticipated
- Public education on the dangers of lightning

Goal 2. Protection of critical facilities & infrastructure
- Grounding of electrical equipment

Goal 3. Protection of personal property and reduction of economic injury due to hazards
- Grounding or disconnecting sources of electrical equipment
section_path: Tornados and High Thunderstorm Winds AND Lightning

doc_title: Natural hazard mitigation plan Jefferson County
section: Mitigation Approaches – Hail, Winter Storm, Extreme Heat, Earthquake, Expansive Soils, Wildfire, Drought and All Hazards
text:
Hail

Goal 1. Protection of critical facilities & infrastructure
- Stronger roofs

Goal 2. Protection of personal property and reduction of economic injury due to hazards
- Stronger roofs
- Protective storage for property

Winter Storm

Goal 1. Protection from loss of life and personal injury
- Provide water, food and shelter for those whose homes are without electric power for days

Goal 2. Protection of critical facilities & infrastructure
- Provide backup electrical power for vital services
- Underground electric lines

Goal 3. Protection of personal property and reduction of economic injury due to hazards
- Warning the public of a predicted ice/snow storm
- Provide backup electrical power for vital services
- Clear snow from highways and spread salt and sand on iced highways
- Underground electric lines

Extreme Heat

Goal 1. Protection from loss of life and personal injury
- Check on frail people during heat waves and ensure their living quarters have working air conditioning or fans

Goal 2. Protection of personal property and reduction of economic injury due to hazards
- Water rationing
- More water treatment and storage capacity

Earthquake

Goal 1. Protection of critical facilities and infrastructure
- Inspection of buildings, roads bridges and dams after earthquake event

Expansive Soils

Goal 1. Protection of critical facilities and infrastructure
- Testing of soils to determine their physical characteristics
- Designing structures to withstand the "worst possible" changing soil conditions as indicated by testing

Goal 2. Protection of personal property and reduction of economic injury due to hazard
- Testing of soils to determine their physical characteristics
- Designing structures to withstand the "worst possible" changing soil conditions as indicated by testing
- Educating building owners/occupants about the soil situation and its potential significance, especially relative to the role of water

Wildfire

Goal 1. Protection from loss of life and personal injury
- Evacuate persons whose homes are in wildfire danger

Goal 2. Protection of personal property and reduction of economic injury due to hazards
- Wildland fire suppression units
- Vegetation control
- Create defensible space
- Water sources for fire suppression

Drought (Hydrologic)

Goal 1. Protection of personal property and reduction of economic injury due to hazards
- Use historic low rainfall in water supply planning
- Develop multiple water supplies
- Develop additional water supply
- Water rationing
- Cloud seeding

All Hazards

Goal 1. Minimize the costs of disaster response
- Public awareness and education programs
- Mitigation plans
section_path: Mitigation Approaches – Hail, Winter Storm, Extreme Heat, Earthquake, Expansive Soils, Wildfire, Drought and All Hazards

doc_title: Natural hazard mitigation plan Jefferson County
section: 3.3 -Action Items: Flooding, Tornados, Severe Thunderstorm, Ice Storm
text:
3.3 – Action Items

This section identifies specific actions to achieve these goals, an appropriate lead person for each action, a relative schedule, and funding sources.

The Jefferson County Hazard Mitigation Planning Committee reviewed the list of practical actions and projects. Those listed below were determined to be those most practical and beneficial in hazard reduction to the Jefferson County.

Flooding items:

Action Item #1 – Develop baseline for existing development in flood plains.

1. Obtain higher resolution, color aerial photographs of county.
2. Locate buildings and facilities inside 100-year flood plain developing photos with flood boundary overlay.
3. Record findings to create baseline for flood plain development.
4. Provide findings and map sheets to flood plain management board.

Responsibility: Association South Central Oklahoma Governments  
Budget: Aerial photos $35,000 (ASCOG), staff time (ASCOG)

Action Item #2 – County Floodplain Board

- Staff and facility support of Jefferson County Floodplain Board. This board reviews proposed building sites to determine if the new residential buildings in the Special Flood Hazard Area have their lowest floor at or above the base flood elevation.

Responsibility: Jefferson County Emergency Management  
Budget: Staff time (operating funds)

Action Item #3 – Flood warnings, barricades and evacuation

- Warn residents in flood areas of impending flood and evacuation if appropriate.
- Temporary barricades of low water crossings during flash floods.

Responsibility: Jefferson County Commissioners  
Budget: Staff time (operating funds)

Tornados and Severe Thunderstorm items:

Action Item #4 – Tornado and severe thunderstorm weather watch and warnings.

1. Maintaining operation of emergency management office during periods of forecasted severe weather to receive reports severe weather.
2. Coordinating training, deployment and reporting of storm watchers.
3. Relay of reports of impending severe weather to local law enforcement agencies.

Responsibility: Jefferson County Emergency Management  
Budget: Staff time (operating funds)

Action Item #5 – Safe rooms at county barns

- Providing shelter for county employees at work place.

Responsibility: Jefferson County Commissioners  
Budget: Capital outlay road funds

Ice Storm items:

Action Item #6 – Generators for emergency electric power.

- Provide necessary generators

Responsibility: Jefferson County Commissioners  
Budget: Grants or federal property loan
section_path: 3.3 -Action Items: Flooding, Tornados, Severe Thunderstorm, Ice Storm

doc_title: Natural hazard mitigation plan Jefferson County
section: 3.3-Action Items: Wildfire, Earthquake, Dam Safety, Other
text:
Wildfire items:

Action Item #7 – Rural Fire Department Support

1. Responding with county equipment during fire emergencies.
2. Helping small fire departments seek and secure grants.
3. Encouragement of county fire association. This group is to be a forum for after-action review of events at fires requiring mutual aid.

Responsibility: Jefferson County  
Budget: Staff time (operating funds)

Action Item #8 – Controlled burns association

1. Affiliation of landowners for the purpose of conducting controlled burns of brush cover land to improve grazing and reduce fuel load in case of wildfire.
2. Furnish expertise, training and equipment for controlled burns.
3. Fire breaks around areas.

Responsibility: ASCOG RC&D  
Budget: Staff time (operating funds)

Earthquake items:

Action Item #9 – Inspection of for damage after earthquake

1. Build list of buildings, road bridges and dams to be inspected for damage immediately after a strong earthquake.

Responsibility: Jefferson County Emergency Management  
Budget: Staff time (operating funds)

Dam Safety items:

Action Item #10 – Inspection of conservation district flood control dams

1. Make available annual dam safety inspection reports.

Responsibility: Jefferson County Conservation District  
Budget: Staff time (operating funds)

Other items:

Action Item #11 – Hazard mitigation and response committee

1. Create a committee made up of city-county-private citizens specifically charged with the responsibility of plan maintenance and solicitation of public input.

Responsibility: Jefferson County Emergency Management  
Budget: Staff time (operating funds)
section_path: 3.3-Action Items: Wildfire, Earthquake, Dam Safety, Other

doc_title: Natural hazard mitigation plan Jefferson County
text:
4 – Plan Maintenance

Jefferson County will ensure that regular review and update of the Hazard Mitigation Plan occurs. The County has formed a Hazard Mitigation Plan Evaluation Committee that consists of members from the local elected officials and other concerned parties, including the County Office of Emergency Management, the Association of South Central Oklahoma Governments and the general public. The Plan Evaluation Committee is responsible for contacting committee members and organizing the annual meeting. The meeting will be held in June of each year, and committee members will be responsible for monitoring and evaluating the progress of the mitigation strategies in the Plan.

The committee will review each goal and objective to determine their relevance to changing situations in the County, as well as changes in State or Federal policy, and to ensure that they are addressing current and expected conditions. The committee will also review the risk assessment portion of the Plan to determine if this information should be updated or modified. The parties responsible for the various implementation actions will report on the status of their projects and will include which implementation processes worked well, any difficulties encountered, how coordination efforts were proceeding, and which strategies should be revised.

The Planning Committee will then have three months to update and make changes to the Plan before submitting it to the Committee members and the State Hazard Mitigation Officer. If no changes are necessary, the State Hazard Mitigation Officer will be given a justification for this determination.

Continued Public Involvement

Jefferson County is dedicated to involving the public directly in the continual reshaping and updating of the Hazard Mitigation Plan. The Hazard Mitigation Plan Evaluation Committee members are responsible for the annual review and update of the Plan. Although they represent the public to some extent, the public will be able to directly comment on and provide feedback about the Plan.

Copies of the Plan will be catalogued and kept on hand at all of the public libraries in the County. The Plan will also be available by website.

Contained in the plan is the postal address, e-mail address and phone numbers of the persons responsible for keeping track of public comments on the Plan.

In addition, copies of the plan and any proposed changes will be posted on the ASCOG website. This site will also contain an email address and phone number to which people can direct their comments or concerns.

A public meeting will also be held after each annual Mitigation Plan Evaluation Committee meeting. This meeting will provide the public a forum for which they can express its concerns, opinions, or ideas about the Plan. The Jefferson County Commissioners will publicize and host this meeting.

doc_title: Natural hazard mitigation plan Jefferson County
section: Appendix – General Rain Flooding Event Descriptions
text:
APPENDIX

Descriptions of hazards events that are representative of what has or could happen.

General Rain Flooding Event Descriptions

• October 13–16, 1923 – Floodwaters from the North Canadian River produced extensive damage in downtown Oklahoma City.

• October 17–21, 1983 – Moisture from the remains of a Pacific hurricane combined with a frontal system to produce widespread rainfall of 6–15 inches across southern, central, and northeast Oklahoma, leading to extensive flooding. There were record discharges on Deep Red Run near Randlett, Washita River at Chickasha and Alex, East Cache Creek near Walters, and Walnut Creek at Purcell. Serious flooding was evident in the lower Washita River Basin. A total of 17 in. of rain fell on the Rush Springs area, with 24-hour totals greater than 8.5 in.

• Late September and Early October 1986 – Remnants of two Pacific tropical cyclones combined to produce widespread rainfall totals of 10–20 inches across northern Oklahoma, leading to record river flooding.

• May 27–29, 1987 – Thunderstorms climaxed 11 days of intense rains in central and southwestern Oklahoma, with 10 to 15 in. of rain falling on some areas. There were record discharges at the Paus Valley and Dicks gages. More than 200 floodwater-retarding structures built by the Natural Resources Conservation Service had emergency spillway discharge. There was severe damage to the cities of Chickasha, Lindsay, and Pauls Valley.
section_path: Appendix – General Rain Flooding Event Descriptions

doc_title: Natural hazard mitigation plan Jefferson County
text:
Flash Flood Event Descriptions

• June 23–24, 1948 – Unofficial rainfall of over 20 inches in a few hours led to 11 drownings on Highway 66 near Hydro.

• October 20, 1973 – Rainfall of over 15 inches in Enid led to nine deaths.

• August 27–28, 1977 – The flash flood in west Cache and Blue Beaver Creeks in southwestern Oklahoma was the result of a severe thunderstorm; runoff was 2,210 cubic feet per second in the upstream reach of Blue Beaver Creek. Rainfall data indicated 24-hour totals of 12 in. immediately south of Cache; an area-weighted average rainfall of 7.7 in. fell during a 6-hour storm period within a 200 square mile area, illustrating the large quantity of water that can result from an Oklahoma thunderstorm.

• May 26–27, 1984 – Rainfall of up to 15 inches (probably more as many gages overflowed) pounded the Tulsa area overnight, leading to 14 deaths.

• October 24, 1994, 10 AM – Heavy rain caused flash flooding in Comanche County where 6 to 12 inches of water were reported over roads southwest of Sterling.

• August 28, 1996, 3:50 AM – Water 2.5 feet in depth covered US HWY 281 nine miles north of Lawton.

• August 28, 1996, 4:45 PM – Water to a depth as high as car windshields at intersection of 52nd and Rogers Lane in Lawton.

• July 10, 1997, 6 PM – Localized heavy rain resulted in the flash flooding of Tony Creek and its tributaries, stranding several motorists and residents in the Fletcher and Elgin areas.

doc_title: Natural hazard mitigation plan Jefferson County
section: Appendix – Tornado and Thunderstorm Wind Event Descriptions
text:
Tornado Event Descriptions

• May 10, 1905 – Snyder Tornado – The death toll of 97 makes this F5 the 2nd-deadliest tornado in state history.

• April 9, 1947 – The most deadly tornado in Oklahoma history killed 181 people, 116 in Woodward alone.

• April 12, 1945 – Antlers Tornado – The death toll of 69 makes this F5 the third deadliest tornado in state history.

• April 26, 1961 – An outbreak of strong and violent tornadoes across northern Oklahoma occurred on this date.

• April 10, 1979 – Red River Valley Tornado – The thunderstorm system that produced the Vernon, TX tornado crossed the Red River and left a 50-mile-long skipping track of tornado damage through Oklahoma. Just after 5 PM another tornado (F4) spawned by the same thunderstorm system crashed into Lawton. Lawton officials sounded the siren system to warn the people of the approaching storm. As a result of the early warning, the casualty list of 3 deaths and 109 injured were relatively small despite the destruction.

• May 3, 1999 – 14 tornadoes reported in a 7-hour period. Beginning north of Fort Sill, the tornadoes traveled across Caddo and Grady and McClain Counties into the Oklahoma City metropolitan area and beyond. The 1st tornado (F0) of the outbreak touched down on US 62, 2 miles north of Interstate 44 in Comanche County at 3:51 PM. No damage is believed to have occurred. The 2nd tornado (F0) formed approximately 3 miles west of Elgin in Comanche County. The most notable tornado was rated F5 and formed over Grady County near Amber and tracked northeast for 37 miles eventually into the Oklahoma City metropolitan area after 6 PM. Bridgecreek, Oklahoma City, Moore, Del City, and Midwest City suffered tremendous damage. 36 direct fatalities and 583 direct injuries were recorded.

Thunderstorm Wind Event Descriptions

• May 22, 1996, 7 PM – An area of thunderstorms over southwest Oklahoma began to collapse and evolved into a massive heatburst with straight-line winds in excess of 70 mph that spread over an area about 150 miles wide from Jackson County to Murray County. The entire heatburst event lasted for about 6 hours. Early morning temperatures rose from the 80s into the low 100s in some places. An estimated 90 percent of houses and buildings in eastern Comanche and western Stephens Counties sustained damage. Downed power lines sparked grass fires. Sustained winds of 80 mph and gusts of 95 mph were measured in Lawton. Streets and highways were closed due to debris and power lines over the road. In Geronimo, eight homes sustained damage, including one home that was destroyed.

• June 15, 1996, 5:30 PM – Thunderstorm winds blew a trailer over in Cache.

• May 7, 1997, 09:50 – Binger – Severe thunderstorm winds estimated at up to 90 mph caused widespread damage and one injury in Binger. One man was injured when his mobile home was overturned. The roof was blown off the grandstand at the local fairgrounds and steel posts were pulled from concrete at a ranch. A farm implement business sustained major damage to both its building and some of the equipment. The roof was completely removed from the building and several combines were blown into each other. A combine and hopper were blown across a highway, about 40 yards from their original location.

• January 4, 1998, 12:30 AM – Thunderstorm winds blew the steeple off a church roof in Lawton.

• November 9, 1998, 8:20 PM – A mobile home was destroyed, and barns damaged 4 miles south of Sterling.

• March 18, 1998, 10:30 PM – 2 Miles North of Dibble – Severe thunderstorm winds destroyed a mobile home, injuring one of the three occupants. Three other homes in the area were damaged and two mobile homes were blown off of their blocks. Large trees were also split.

• May 31, 1999, 8:55 PM – Damaging straight-line winds developed in southern Kiowa County and expanded east to cover large areas of western and central Comanche County. Wind speeds reached 110 mph to 130 mph in the Pecos Valley subdivision located west of Lawton. Across Lawton and Fort Sill, wind speeds decreased, but still caused tree, power line, and roof damage. In total, 9 houses were destroyed; 58 houses and 8 businesses sustained major damage; and 150 houses sustained minor damage in Comanche County. Three injuries were reported.
section_path: Appendix – Tornado and Thunderstorm Wind Event Descriptions

massive heatburst with straight-line winds in excess of 70 mph that spread over an area about 150 miles wide
from Jackson County to Murray County. The entire heatburst event lasted for about 6 hours. Early morning
temperatures rose from the 80s into the low 100s in some places. An estimated 90 percent of houses and
outbuildings in eastern Comanche and western Stephens Counties sustained damage. Downed power lines
sparked grass fires. Sustained winds of 80 mph and gusts of 95 mph were measured in Lawton. Streets and
highways were closed due to debris and power lines over the road. In Geronimo, eight homes sustained
damage, including one mobile home that was destroyed.

farm implement business sustained major damage to both its bUilding and to some of the equipment. The roof

o January 4, 1998, 12:30 AM Thunderstorm winds blew the steeple off a church roof in Lawton.
o November 9, 1998,8:20 PM - A mobile home was destroyed, and barns were damaged 4 miles south of
Sterling. March 18, 1998, 10:30 PM - 2 Miles North of Dibble - Severe thunderstorm winds destroyed a mobile
home, injuring one of the three occupants. Three other homes in the area were damaged and two mobile
homes were blown off of their blocks. Large trees were also split.
o May 31, 1999,8:55 PM - Damaging straight-line winds developed in southern Kiowa County and expanded in
coverage as they moved eastward. This swath of damaging winds, at times 10 to 15 miles wide, moved east
across western and central Comanche County. Wind speeds reached 110 mph to 130 mph in the Pecan Valley
subdivision located west of Lawton. Across Lawton and Fort Sill, wind speeds decreased, but still caused tree,

o June 24, 1999,5:30 PM - Three main power feeder lines were blown down in Lawton.
o June 30, 1999,5:30 AM - A tractor trailer was blown over, and the wall of a cafe was damaged in Elgin.
o August 12, 1999,5:45 PM - Several trees were downed, and minor roof damage occurred to several homes in
Lawton.
o October 29, 1999,9 PM - Dozens of utility lines were downed in Cache, and trees were downed in Elgin.
o March 28, 2000, 7 PM - Four mobile homes were destroyed and two others were damaged in Geronimo.
o May 4,2001,4:20 AM - 3 Miles West North West of Walters - Two mobile homes were destroyed, with one of
them occupied by a family of 3 at the time. The 3 family members were taken to the hospital with minor

being broken ..
o May 27, 2001, 10:05 PM - A telephone pole fell on and killed a passenger riding on a motorcycle in Lawton.
o Jun 15, 2002, 9 PM - Grandfield - Part of a restaurant roof blew off. .
o April 23, 2003, 7 PM - Waurika - Straight-line winds caused roof damage to 12 homes, uprooted or destroyed
many trees, broke several windows, damaged an outbuilding at a golf course and damaged some signs at the
Richard Football Field. The strong winds cut a damage path a quarter of a mile wide and 2 miles long through
the town.

doc_title: Natural hazard mitigation plan Jefferson County
text:
o July 23, 1994, 9:35 AM - Lightning damaged computers and telephone lines at Fort Sill.
o May 3, 1995,3:30 AM - Lightning struck a stone tower at the Wichita Mountain Wildlife Refuge. The strike blew
the battlement apart and set fire to the roof beams.
o May 13, 1995, 6:00 PM - Lightning killed a man who was on top of a silo four miles north of Kansas, OK
o June 23, 1995, 11:40 AM - Lightning killed a man seven miles north of Red Oak.
o September 12, 1995, 10 PM - Lightning struck an apartment building in Lawton, igniting and damaging the roof.
o June 16, 1996, 8 PM - Lightning struck the side of an apartment building in Lawton, broke a 2-foot hole in the
wall.
o July 16, 1995, 9: 15 PM - Lightning struck the support pole of a tent on Fort Sill Army post, injuring all 26
occupants. Most injuries were cuts and bruises from the collapse of the tent.
o July 8, 1996, 6: 15 PM - Lightning killed a man on top of railroad car in Guymon.
o July 21, 1997, 8:30 PM - Lightning killed a man when he went outdoors to roll up his car windows in Claremore.
o August 18, 1997, 5 PM -Lightning killed a man riding a horse at Black Mesa State Park.
o August 9, 1998, 7 AM - Lightning killed a woman fishing at Sequoyah Bay State Park.
o April 24, 1999, 7:30 AM - Lightning killed a man fishing in a tournament on the Arkansas River near Lock and
Dam 14.
o June 11, 1998, 12:30 AM - Lightning struck and set fire to 2 buildings at an old amusement park in Cache.
o August 10, 1998, 5:33 PM - Lightning struck 2 oil tanks 3 miles east of Sterling setting them ablaze.
o October 22, 2000, 5: 15 PM - Lightning struck an insulator in Lawton causing a power outage to 1100 homes.
o January 16, 2001, 9 AM - Lightning struck the ground near a group of 34 soldiers training at the East Range at
Fort Sill, sending 5 of them to the hospital for treatment.
o September 3, 2001,4 PM - Lightning struck a storage facility complex at Sexton Office Supplies, in Apache
causing a fire that consumed four buildings.

doc_title: Natural hazard mitigation plan Jefferson County
text:
Hail Event Descriptions
o April 19, 1996, 7:24 PM - Wright City - One man suffered from a broken toe as a hail stone fell on his foot.
o June 8, 1998,4:20 PM - Ponca City -A man and an elderly woman were injured when they were caught outside
in the hailstorm. The woman was taken to a local hospital and treated for cuts and bruises from the hail and a
fractured hip.
o May 3,1999,4:13 PM - 5 miles north of Altus - Hail to the size of softballs (4.5 inches) smashed hundreds of
vehicles and damaged many buildings 5 miles north of Altus. At least 400 individual claims were received by
local insurance offices with total damage near $800,000, and in Weatherford, hail to the size of baseballs (2.75
inches) dented vehicles and broke many windows.
o April 15, 2000, 5:26 PM - Altus - Baseball size hail (2.75 inches in diameter) caused an estimated 2 million
dollars in damage to homes and businesses and 600 thousand dollars to vehicles. Severe thunderstorms
affected a large part of western Oklahoma during the afternoon and evening of the 15th before the storms
eventually weakened across central Oklahoma. The most significant damage resulted from baseball size hail
which fell in Altus in Jackson County, with an estimated $2.6 million in damage.
o September 17, 2001, 7:45 PM - Hooker - Damage was reported to vehicles parked outside and also sidings
and roofs were full of holes along with many broken windows. :
o April 13, 2002, 6:30 AM - 6 miles southwest of Grandfield - Hail damage was sustained to vehicles and trees.

doc_title: Natural hazard mitigation plan Jefferson County
text:
Ice Storm Event Descriptions
o December 25-27, 1987 - Ice accumulations up to 2 inches, from near Duncan to Norman to Tulsa, left many
areas without power for a week or more.
o January 5-7, 1988 - Significant snowfall amounts were reported across Oklahoma. The storm totals exceeded
6-inches over Virtually the entire state, except a few areas near the Red River and the far western Oklahoma
Panhandle.
o November 24, 1996 - Ice accumulated up to 1/2 inch thick mainly southeast of a line from Shawnee to
Chickasha to Frederick. Power was out to a large portion of the area due to icing of power lines and tree limbs.
It took as long as 3 days to restore power to some customers.
o December 20, 1998 - Light freeZing rain produced a thin layer of ice on most roads. Across the entire state,
there were 13 fatal traffic accidents and 100 injury-related traffic accidents.
o January 7, 1999 - Light freezing rain and freezing drizzle and dense fog. Hundreds of traffic accidents were
reported, including 3 fatal accidents. Many schools were closed. Power lines were downed due to the
accumulation of ice in Lawton, where 50 homes were without power on the 8th.
o December 26, 2000 - Ice accumulations of about 1 inch. Damage to property was extensive in a band from
Randlett and Walters in Cotton County northeastward through Duncan, Ringling, Waurika and beyond. Tens of
thousands of residents were left without electricity for nearly a week, while thousands of residents were
without water or phone service for several days. Thousands of homes and vehicles were damaged by falling
trees and ice, while thousands of trees and utility poles were damaged or destroyed.
o January 30, 2002 - Ice accumulations of 1 to 2 inches. The worst damage occurred in a 60-mile wide band,
extending from near Ponca City southwestward toward Anadarko and Hobart. Dozens of towns were left
completely without power for days, with some residents without power for weeks. The damage was
catastrophic in places, with thousands of utility poles, along with thousands of trees,
were brought down by the weight of the ice.

doc_title: Natural hazard mitigation plan Jefferson County
text:
June 27, 1994 – The high temperature of 120 degrees from the Oklahoma Mesonet four miles south of Tipton tied the record for highest temperatures ever recorded in the state. Additional high temperatures included 116 in Chattanooga and 115 in Frederick.

July 1996 – During this prolonged period of hot temperatures, 7 deaths were attributed to excessive heat. All of the victims were elderly and all but one were in their homes without air conditioning.

Summer 1998 – Excessive heat affected Oklahoma from May through September causing 19 fatalities.

July 1999 – A period of very hot temperatures affected portions of central and southwest Oklahoma from July 26th through July 31st. Eight persons died from the excessive heat.

July 27 – A 57-year-old man was found dead in his home in Norman.  
July 28 – A 65-year-old woman found dead in her home in Shawnee.  
July 28 – A 73-year-old woman collapsed and died while mowing her lawn in Altus.  
July 30 – A 73-year-old man was found dead in his home in Ponca City.  
July 30 – An 84-year-old woman found dead in her home in Oklahoma City.  
July 30 – An 85-year-old woman found dead in her home in Oklahoma City.  
July 30 – An 86-year-old woman found dead in her home in Oklahoma City.  
July 30 – An 82-year-old woman found dead in her home in Oklahoma City.

Except for the fatality in Altus, the majority of persons who died from the heat owned an air conditioner, but it wasn't being used, and the windows of their homes were closed.

August 28, 2000 – A 74-year-old woman was found dead in her home in south Oklahoma City due to heat exhaustion. The control switch for the central air heating and cooling system was set in the heat position rather than the cool position.

August 29, 2000 – An 80-year-old woman was found dead in her apartment in Norman due to heat exhaustion.

September 2, 2000 – An 85-year-old man was found dead in his Oklahoma City home on north Hudson due to excessive heat. Reportedly, the heater was accidentally turned on when intending to turn on the air conditioner.

July 2001 – An extended period of excessive heat affected all of western and central Oklahoma in July. Nine fatalities resulted from the heat.

July 6 – A 78-year-old male died in Stillwater while loading equipment at a storage facility.  
July 14 – An 82-year-old female died in her residence in Oklahoma City.  
July 15 – A 62-year-old male died in his residence in Edmond.  
July 17 – A 29-year-old male died in an Oklahoma City parking lot.  
July 21 – A 76-year-old male died in a residence in Lawton.  
July 21 – A man died from heat stroke in rural Pawnee County.  
July 21 – An 85-year-old female died in her residence in Oklahoma City.  
July 24 – A 52-year-old male died in his residence in Oklahoma City.  
July 28 – 49-year-old male died in his residence in Oklahoma City.  
July 17, 2001 – One man in rural Pawnee County died from heat stroke.

doc_title: Natural hazard mitigation plan Jefferson County
section: Appendix – Drought and Earthquake Event Descriptions
text:
Drought Event Descriptions

Dust Bowl – Early and Mid-1930s – The Dust Bowl of the 1930s ranks among the most significant events of the century nationally, changing the face of the Great Plains. Extreme heat and drought, especially in 1934 and 1936. “Black Sunday” was near the height of the Dust Bowl, with accounts of giant clouds of dust descending on Hooker, OK April 14, 1935.

Mid 1950s – The 1950s drought was characterized by both low rainfall amounts and excessively high temperatures. It devastated the region’s agriculture. Crop yields in some areas dropped as much as 50% and grasslands typically used for grazing were scorched.

August 2000 – Comanche County was declared a federal drought disaster area by the U.S. Department of Agriculture in the 1999–2000 season. An extended period of unusually dry weather in early August lasted for two months. Many parts of the state did not receive rain in August, with portions of southwest and south central Oklahoma remaining dry for almost 90 days starting in June. Total agricultural losses estimated between $600 million and $1 billion statewide. Carter, Comanche, Cotton, Jefferson, Love, Marshall and Tillman Counties were declared federal disaster areas. Reservoir levels were also low across southwest and south central Oklahoma averaging 50 percent of normal.

Earthquake Event Descriptions

October 22, 1882 – Near Fort Gibson – The Cherokee Advocate newspaper reported that “the trembling and vibrating were so severe as to cause doors and window shutters to open and shut, hogs in pens to fall and squeal, poultry to run and hide, the tops of weeds to dip, cattle to lower.”April 9, 1952 - Epicenter near EI Reno. Magnitude 5.5. The largest known Oklahoma earthquake, with the
possible exception of the 1882 earthquake. This earthquake caused the taller buildings in Oklahoma City to
sway and was felt in Austin, TX as well as Des Moines, lA.
February 8 2002 - Epicenter 5 miles west and 1 mile north of Elgin. Magnitude 3.8. Observation from near the
epicenter: "I was sitting at my desk in our home office doing paper work and listening to Ft. Sill practice
bombing. Then Iheard a bombing that lasted 4 times longer and louder when the shaking started and it
seemed that it would never stop. At first Ithought Ft. Sill had put too much dynamite into the last bomb but it
just kept getting louder and the shaking started. Our house shakes when Ft. Sill is practicing but you get used
to it. This you could definitely tell was not the base. My whole desk was shaking as well as myself in the chair.
The entire house shook for at least a minute startling my daughter. Nothing fell off the walls but the chandelier
was swaying in the dining room where my daughter was and some of the pictures on the wall were crooked I
didn't notice anything before this quake but Itold my daughter that this one felt a lot stronger than the one I
felt in 1998 at this same location. The one in 1998 was like a rubber band -- one or two shakes back and forth
-- but it was just as loud."
